result.claim: The study uses the CoQA dataset.
result.original_text: Using the CoQA dataset and the Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 language model, we propose and evaluate three distinct approaches for predicting a user's next questions within a dialogue.

result.claim: The evaluation measures the expected value of the percentage of successfully predicted test questions.
result.original_text: Our evaluation, which measures the expected value of the percentage of successfully predicted test questions, reveals that the topic-andaspect-driven first approach (EV=3,1%) outperforms both the multi-dialogue cluster-based approach (EV=2,4%) and the direct context-based approach (EV=2,4%).
result.section_name: An Approach for RAG Performance Improvement Based on User Requests Predicting and Interests Modelling

result.claim: The dataset contains 7199 dialogues with different number of questions each.
result.original_text: The dataset contains 7199 dialogues with different number of questions each.

result.claim: Questions are interconnected, often relying on context from previous turns in the dialogue.
result.original_text: Questions are interconnected, often relying on context from previous turns in the dialogue.

result.claim: The study specifically analyzes the subset of dialogues containing exactly 20 questions.
result.original_text: To conduct a controlled experiment, this study specifically analyzes the subset of dialogues containing exactly 20 questions.

result.claim: The questions are divided into sections: the first 60% of questions (12) are used to feed the model, while the remaining 40% (8) are used later as test questions.
result.original_text: To do this, the questions are divided into sections: the first 60% of questions (in this case, 12) will be used to feed the model, while the remaining 40% (8) will be used later as test questions (Fig. 1).
result.value: first 60% (12) as prompt, remaining 40% (8) as test

result.claim: The prompt questions (PQ) are passed to the LLM as a prompt, and the model outputs the topic of the dialogue.
result.original_text: So, the PQ are passed to the LLM as a prompt, and the model outputs the topic of the dialogue.

result.claim: The previously identified topics and the same 12 questions are fed into the LLM's prompt.
result.original_text: To do this, the previously identified topics and the same 12 questions are fed into the LLM's promt.

result.claim: The identified topics, aspects, and the first 60% of questions are passed to the LLM's prompt to generate questions.
result.original_text: To generate questions, the identified topics, aspects, and the first 60% of questions are passed to the LLM's prompt.

result.claim: The generated questions are compared with the evaluate questions (EQ) using a LLM-as-a-judge technique.
result.original_text: This is done using a LLM-as-a-judge technique that compares each question to each other.

result.claim: The comparison uses a scale from 0 to 1, where 0.5 is "Possibly equivalent (similar intent but different phrasing)".
result.original_text: The comparison uses a scale from 0 to 1, where 0.5 is "Possibly equivalent (similar intent but different phrasing)".

result.claim: Pairs of questions that yield a result no less than this threshold are considered similar.
result.original_text: Therefore, pairs of questions that yield a result no less than this threshold are considered similar.

result.claim: 1000 dialogues were considered to simulate the scenario with similar topics.
result.original_text: To simulate this scenario, where we have dialogues with similar topics, 1000 dialogues were considered.

result.claim: Each dialogue contains exactly 20 questions.
result.original_text: Each of these dialogues also contains exactly 20 questions.

result.claim: The topic of each dialogue was extracted using an LLM based on the dialogue context.
result.original_text: Based on the context of the dialogue, the topic of the dialogue was extracted using LLM.

result.claim: DBSCAN was used to group the dialogue topics into clusters.
result.original_text: Next, using DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise), the topics of the dialogues were grouped into clusters.

result.claim: This approach additionally uses PQ from the remaining dialogues in the cluster to identify aspects, beyond the topic and PQ of the dialogue under consideration.
result.original_text: However, whereas the previous approach used topic and PQ from the dialogue under consideration, this approach additionally uses PQ from the remaining dialogues in the cluster to identify aspects.
result.value: PQ from remaining dialogues in the cluster

result.claim: Question generation uses the topics, aspects, and PQ of the dialogue under consideration as well as the aspects and PQ of other dialogues in the same cluster.
result.original_text: Next, to generate questions, the topics, aspects and PQ of the dialogue under consideration, as well as aspects and PQ of other dialogues in the cluster, are used.
result.value: topics, aspects, PQ of dialogue and aspects, PQ of other dialogues

result.claim: The generated questions are compared with the EQ for each dialogue separately at the end of the procedure.
result.original_text: At the end, the same procedure is carried out to compare the generated questions with the EQ for each dialogue separately.

result.claim: The third approach eliminates the step of identifying topic and aspects of the dialogue.
result.original_text: The third approach differs from the previous ones in that it eliminates the step of identifying topic and aspects of the dialogue.
result.value: eliminates step of identifying topic and aspects

result.claim: Questions are generated based on the context found in each dialogue in the CoQA dataset.
result.original_text: Instead, questions are generated based on the context found in each dialogue in the CoQA dataset.

result.original_text: The dialog context and the PQ are passed to the LLM's prompt for question generation.

result.claim: The same question comparison procedure is then performed.
result.original_text: The same question comparison procedure is then performed.

result.original_text: For approaches 1 and 3, 100 identical dialogues with 20 questions were considered.

result.claim: The second approach used a total of 85 dialogues grouped into 20 clusters.
result.original_text: For the second approach, a total of 85 dialogues were used, grouped into 20 clusters.

result.original_text: EV = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{T_i}{M} \cdot 100\% \right)

result.claim: The model was fed the full context of the user's questions along with PQ.
result.original_text: Even though the model was fed the full context of the user's questions along with PQ, it was unable to reliably predict the questions.

step3_selection.claim: The study uses the CoQA dataset.
The study uses the Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 language model.
The study proposes three distinct approaches for predicting a user's next questions within a dialogue.
The first approach identifies the dialogue's core topic and its aspects from initial user questions to guide generation.
The second approach incorporates information from topically similar dialogues via clustering to enrich the generation context.
The third approach bypasses explicit topic modeling, generating questions directly from the source text narrative.
The evaluation measures the expected value of the percentage of successfully predicted test questions.
The dataset contains 7199 dialogues with different number of questions each.
Questions are interconnected, often relying on context from previous turns in the dialogue.
The study specifically analyzes the subset of dialogues containing exactly 20 questions.
The questions are divided into sections: the first 60% of questions (12) are used to feed the model, while the remaining 40% (8) are used later as test questions.
The prompt questions (PQ) are passed to the LLM as a prompt, and the model outputs the topic of the dialogue.
The previously identified topics and the same 12 questions are fed into the LLM's prompt.
The identified topics, aspects, and the first 60% of questions are passed to the LLM's prompt to generate questions.
The generated questions are compared with the evaluate questions (EQ) using a LLM-as-a-judge technique.
The comparison uses a scale from 0 to 1, where 0.5 is "Possibly equivalent (similar intent but different phrasing)".
Pairs of questions that yield a result no less than this threshold are considered similar.
1000 dialogues were considered to simulate the scenario with similar topics.
Each dialogue contains exactly 20 questions.
The topic of each dialogue was extracted using an LLM based on the dialogue context.
DBSCAN was used to group the dialogue topics into clusters.
This approach additionally uses PQ from the remaining dialogues in the cluster to identify aspects, beyond the topic and PQ of the dialogue under consideration.
Question generation uses the topics, aspects, and PQ of the dialogue under consideration as well as the aspects and PQ of other dialogues in the same cluster.
The generated questions are compared with the EQ for each dialogue separately at the end of the procedure.
The third approach eliminates the step of identifying topic and aspects of the dialogue.
Questions are generated based on the context found in each dialogue in the CoQA dataset.
The dialog context and the PQ are passed to the LLM's prompt for question generation.
The same question comparison procedure is then performed.
Approaches 1 and 3 considered 100 identical dialogues with 20 questions each.
The second approach used a total of 85 dialogues grouped into 20 clusters.
The Expected Value (EV) is calculated as EV = (1/N) * sum_{i=1}^{N} (T_i / M * 100%).
N is a total number of dialogues.
M is a total EQ per dialogue (8 in our case).
T is a number of successfully predicted test questions in dialogue i.
This metric represents the average percentage of test questions per dialogue that can be predicted.
Approach 2 uses domain and aspects based question generation.
The model was fed the full context of the user's questions along with PQ.
